rules.title: Missing comma before FANBOYS joining independent clauses
rules.description: When a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) joins two independent clauses, a comma is needed before it.
rules.pattern_description: Detect two clauses (each with subject+verb) joined by FANBOYS without a preceding comma. Requires NLP clause boundary detection.

incorrect: She likes coffee but he prefers tea.
I went to the store and I bought milk.
It was raining so we stayed inside.
correct: She likes coffee, but he prefers tea.
I went to the store, and I bought milk.
It was raining, so we stayed inside.

exceptions: Short closely related clauses: 'I came and I saw.' — comma optional.
When joining only subjects/objects: 'She likes coffee and tea.' — NO comma.
rules.fix_hint: Add a comma before the coordinating conjunction when joining two independent clauses.

rules.title: 'Although...but' redundancy
rules.description: Using both a subordinating conjunction and a coordinating conjunction for the same contrast is redundant.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(although|though|even though)\b[^.]*\bbut\b/i, /\bbecause\b[^.]*\bso\b/i, /\b(despite|in spite of)\b[^.]*\bbut\b/i

incorrect: Although she was tired, but she kept working.
Because it was raining, so we stayed inside.
Despite the delay, but we arrived on time.
correct: Although she was tired, she kept working.
Because it was raining, we stayed inside.
Despite the delay, we arrived on time.

rules.fix_hint: Remove one of the two conjunctions — keep the subordinating one or the coordinating one, not both.

rules.title: Correlative conjunction verb agreement
rules.description: With either...or, neither...nor, the verb agrees with the subject nearest to it.
rules.pattern_description: Detect (neither|either) ... (nor|or) patterns and check verb agreement with nearest subject.

incorrect: Neither the teacher nor the students was ready.
Either John or the boys has left.
Neither she nor I is going.
correct: Neither the teacher nor the students were ready.
Either John or the boys have left.
Neither she nor I am going.

rules.fix_hint: Make the verb agree with the subject nearest to it (after nor/or).

rules.title: Conjunctive adverb with only a comma
rules.description: Conjunctive adverbs joining two independent clauses require a semicolon before them (or period + new sentence), plus a comma after.
rules.pattern_description: Detect independent clause + comma + conjunctive adverb (however|therefore|moreover|furthermore|consequently|nevertheless|nonetheless|meanwhile|thus|hence|thereby|otherwise|accordingly|similarly|likewise|indeed|instead|still) + independent clause.

incorrect: She was tired, however she kept working.
He failed the test, therefore he studied harder.
It rained, consequently we stayed home.
correct: She was tired; however, she kept working.
He failed the test; therefore, he studied harder.
It rained; consequently, we stayed home.

exceptions: When used parenthetically mid-sentence: 'She, however, kept working.' (commas are correct).
rules.fix_hint: Replace the comma before the conjunctive adverb with a semicolon or period, and add a comma after it.

rules.title: 'The reason is because' redundancy
rules.description: 'The reason is because' is redundant — 'reason' already implies 'because'.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\bthe\s+reason\s+(is|was|being)\s+because\b/i

incorrect: The reason is because he was late.
The reason was because the system crashed.
The main reason is because costs increased.
correct: The reason is that he was late.
He was late because of the system crash.
The main reason is that costs increased.

exceptions: Widely used in informal speech. Flag only in formal register.
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'because' with 'that' after 'the reason is'.

rules.title: Wordy conjunction replacement
rules.description: Replace verbose conjunctive phrases with simpler equivalents.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\bdue to the fact that\b/i → 'because', /\bin spite of the fact that\b/i → 'although', /\bregardless of the fact that\b/i → 'although', /\bfor the purpose of\b/i → 'to', /\bin order to\b/i → 'to', /\bin the event that\b/i → 'if', /\bin light of the fact that\b/i → 'because', /\bwith the exception of\b/i → 'except'

incorrect: Due to the fact that it rained, we stayed.
In spite of the fact that she was tired, she worked.
In the event that you fail, try again.
correct: Although she was tired, she worked.

exceptions: Some verbose forms are appropriate in legal or very formal writing.
rules.fix_hint: Replace the verbose phrase with the simpler equivalent.

rules.title: Missing comma after introductory element
rules.description: Place a comma after introductory words, phrases, or clauses.
rules.pattern_description: Match sentence-initial introductory adverbs (However, Therefore, Furthermore, Moreover, Nevertheless, Meanwhile, Consequently, Additionally, Similarly, Accordingly, Unfortunately, Fortunately, Finally, Obviously, Clearly, Actually, Basically, Certainly, Generally, Honestly, Naturally, Personally, Seriously, Technically, Typically, Ultimately) NOT followed by a comma.
exceptions: 'However' meaning 'in whatever way': 'However you do it, be careful.' (no comma after however, comma after clause).
rules.fix_hint: Add a comma after the introductory element.

rules.title: Missing comma in direct address
rules.description: Use a comma to set off names or titles when directly addressing someone.
rules.pattern_description: Match greeting/address word (Thanks, Thank you, Hi, Hello, Hey, Dear, Excuse me, Good morning, Good afternoon, Good evening, Please, Sorry, Yes, No, Well, OK, Okay) + proper noun without comma.
rules.fix_hint: Add commas around the name/title being addressed.

rules.title: its vs it's confusion
rules.description: 'It's' = 'it is' or 'it has'. 'Its' = possessive (belonging to it). Never use 'its'' (with trailing apostrophe).
rules.pattern_description: Detect 'its' before a verb (been, is, going, raining, etc.) where 'it's' is needed. Detect 'it's' before a noun where possessive 'its' is needed: 'it's color', 'it's purpose', 'it's role'.
rules.fix_hint: If you can replace with 'it is' or 'it has', use 'it's'. Otherwise, use 'its' (no apostrophe).

rules.title: your vs you're confusion
rules.description: 'You're' = 'you are'. 'Your' = possessive (belonging to you).
rules.pattern_description: Detect 'your' before adjective/adverb describing the listener ('your welcome', 'your right', 'your wrong', 'your amazing', 'your going'). Detect 'you're' before a noun where 'your' is needed ('you're house', 'you're car', 'you're book').
rules.fix_hint: If you can replace with 'you are', use 'you're'. Otherwise 'your'.

rules.title: their/they're/there confusion
rules.description: 'They're' = 'they are'. 'Their' = possessive. 'There' = location/existential.
rules.pattern_description: Detect 'their' + verb (going, coming, working) → 'they're'. Detect 'there' + possessive context ('there house') → 'their'. Detect 'they're' + noun ('they're house') → 'their'.
rules.fix_hint: 'they are' = they're, 'belonging to them' = their, 'that place' = there.

rules.title: Apostrophe in simple plurals
rules.description: Never use an apostrophe to form a regular plural noun.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b([A-Za-z]+)'s\b/ where the word is a regular plural context (not possessive). Detect patterns like 'apple's for sale', 'the dog's are barking', 'DVD's', '1990's'.
exceptions: Apostrophes in plurals of single letters: 'Mind your p's and q's.' Decades: '1990's' is acceptable in some style guides.
rules.fix_hint: Remove the apostrophe — use plain -s for plurals.

rules.title: Semicolon joining independent clauses
rules.description: A semicolon joins two closely related independent clauses without a conjunction.
rules.pattern_description: Detect two independent clauses separated by a period where a semicolon might improve flow, or comma splices that should use a semicolon.

incorrect: She likes coffee, he prefers tea.
The project failed, the budget was exceeded.
correct: She likes coffee; he prefers tea.
The project failed; the budget was exceeded.

exceptions: When clauses are not closely related, use a period instead.
rules.fix_hint: Replace the comma with a semicolon, or add a coordinating conjunction after the comma.

rules.title: Missing hyphen in compound adjective before noun
rules.description: Compound adjectives before a noun should be hyphenated.
rules.pattern_description: Match common compound adjectives without hyphens: 'well known author'→'well-known author', 'high quality product'→'high-quality product', 'long term plan'→'long-term plan', 'full time job'→'full-time job', 'part time work'→'part-time work', 'state of the art technology'→'state-of-the-art technology', 'up to date information'→'up-to-date information', 'real time data'→'real-time data'.
exceptions: After the noun: 'The author is well known.' (no hyphen). Adverb ending -ly: 'a beautifully designed room' (no hyphen).
rules.fix_hint: Add hyphens between the words forming the compound adjective when it precedes the noun.

rules.title: Top 100 misspelled words
rules.description: A dictionary of the most commonly misspelled English words.
rules.pattern_description: Word-boundary regex match for each misspelling → correct form. Includes: accommodate, acquire, argument, beginning, believe, bizarre, calendar, Caribbean, cemetery, committee, conscious, conscience, definitely, dilemma, embarrass, equivalent, exaggerate, existence, foreign, guarantee, harass, hierarchy, humorous, immediately, independent, indispensable, liaison, maintenance, millennium, miniature, miscellaneous, necessary, noticeable, occasionally, occurrence, parallel, perceive, perseverance, personnel, possession, privilege, receive, recommend, referred, relevant, resistance, restaurant, rhythm, schedule, separate, sergeant, similar, sincerely, subtle, supersede, surprise, tendency, threshold, tomorrow, unnecessary, vacuum, Wednesday, weird.
rules.fix_hint: Replace with correct spelling.
rules.british_variant: See SP_BRA_001 for British/American spelling differences.

rules.title: British/American spelling variant detection
rules.description: Detect and flag when British and American spellings are mixed within the same document.
rules.pattern_description: Track occurrences of variant pairs: -our/-or (colour/color, favour/favor, honour/honor, humour/humor, labour/labor, neighbour/neighbor, behaviour/behavior), -re/-er (centre/center, theatre/theater, metre/meter, fibre/fiber, litre/liter, lustre/luster, sombre/somber), -ise/-ize (organise/organize, realise/realize, recognise/recognize, specialise/specialize, analyse/analyze), -ence/-ense (defence/defense, licence/license, offence/offense, pretence/pretense), -ogue/-og (catalogue/catalog, dialogue/dialog, analogue/analog), double/single consonant (travelled/traveled, cancelled/canceled, modelling/modeling, labelled/labeled, counsellor/counselor, jewellery/jewelry), -ae/-e (anaemia/anemia, orthopaedic/orthopedic, paediatric/pediatric). Flag when document contains both variants.

incorrect: The colour of the center was nice.
correct: The color of the center was nice. (AmE)
The colour of the centre was nice. (BrE)

exceptions: Proper nouns: 'Defence Ministry' or 'Department of Defense' — preserve as-is. Direct quotes preserve original spelling.
rules.fix_hint: Maintain consistency: detect which variant is dominant in the document and flag exceptions.
rules.british_variant: This rule specifically handles British/American differences.

rules.title: Incorrect consonant doubling
rules.description: When adding a vowel suffix, double the final consonant only if the word ends CVC AND has stress on the final syllable.
rules.pattern_description: Wrong doubles: writting→writing, begining→beginning, occured→occurred, refered→referred, comming→coming, openning→opening, happenning→happening, developped→developed, occuring→occurring, prefered→preferred, benefitting→benefiting (AmE), cancelling→canceling (AmE). Wrong singles: runing→running, siting→sitting, planing→planning, stoping→stopping, shoping→shopping, droping→dropping, geting→getting, puting→putting, cuting→cutting, hiting→hitting.
exceptions: British English doubles more consonants: travelled, cancelled, modelling (vs American: traveled, canceled, modeling).
rules.fix_hint: Check the stress pattern and final consonant to determine whether to double.
rules.british_variant: British English doubles final consonant in more words: travelled, modelled, cancelled.

rules.title: I before E rule violations
rules.description: I before E except after C, or when sounded as 'ay' (as in neighbor, weigh).
rules.pattern_description: Match common errors: recieve→receive, beleive→believe, acheive→achieve, concieve→conceive, decieve→deceive, percieve→perceive, wierd→weird, nieghbor→neighbor, foriegn→foreign, seize (correct, exception to rule), their (correct, 'ay' sound).
exceptions: weird, seize, either, neither, foreign, leisure, protein, caffeine, species, science, sufficient, ancient, glacier, society — numerous exceptions exist.
rules.fix_hint: Apply i-before-e rule, but check the exceptions list.

rules.title: Silent E errors with suffixes
rules.description: Drop silent 'e' before a vowel suffix (-ing, -able, -ous, -ation), keep before consonant suffix (-ment, -ful, -less, -ness).
rules.pattern_description: Wrong: makeing→making, hopeing→hoping, useing→using, moveing→moving, liveing→living, writeing→writing, loveable→lovable, arguement→argument, truely→truly, nineth→ninth, judgement→judgment (AmE). Correct: hopeful (keep e), movement (keep e), useful (keep e), barely (keep e).
exceptions: Keep 'e' after soft c/g: noticeable, changeable, manageable, courageous. 'Judgement' accepted in British English.
rules.fix_hint: Drop the 'e' before vowel suffixes; keep before consonant suffixes (with noted exceptions).
rules.british_variant: 'Judgement' is standard in British English. 'Ageing' (BrE) vs 'aging' (AmE).

rules.title: Y to I conversion errors
rules.description: When a word ends in consonant + y, change y to i before most suffixes (but keep y before -ing).
rules.pattern_description: Wrong: happyer→happier, easyer→easier, beautyfull→beautiful, partys→parties, familys→families, carryed→carried, tryed→tried, studyed→studied, cryed→cried, replyed→replied, applyed→applied. Keep y: studying (not studing), carrying (not carriing), trying (not triing), playing (not plaiing — vowel + y stays).
exceptions: Vowel + y: stays as is (played, keys, donkeys). Before -ing: keep y (studying, carrying). Proper nouns (the Kennedys, not Kennedies).
rules.fix_hint: Change consonant + y to i before suffixes other than -ing.

CJ.tokenization: Conjunction errors are mix of regex (redundancy, wordy) and NLP (clause boundary detection for comma rules).
CJ.nlp_requirements: CJ_CRD_001 needs clause boundary detection. CJ_COR_001 needs nearest-subject agreement checking.
CJ.regex_applicability: CJ_RED_001, CJ_RSN_001, CJ_WRD_001 are purely regex-based. CJ_ADV_001 can use regex with clause heuristics.
CJ.performance: Combine wordy conjunction patterns into a single alternation regex for efficiency.

PU.tokenization: Punctuation checks operate at character level. Split text preserving all whitespace and punctuation.
PU.nlp_requirements: Comma rules need clause detection. Apostrophe rules are regex-based with POS context.
PU.regex_applicability: Most PU rules are regex-based. PU_COM_001, PU_APO_001-004 are excellent regex candidates.
PU.performance: Apostrophe checks should run early — they're fast and high-value for user satisfaction.

SP.tokenization: Spelling checks use word-boundary matching. Build a HashMap<misspelling, correction> for O(1) lookup.
SP.nlp_requirements: SP_BRA_001 needs document-level analysis to detect dominant spelling variant. All others are word-level.
SP.regex_applicability: All SP rules are regex-based. SP_COM_001 should use a compiled map of 200+ entries.
SP.performance: Compile all misspellings into a single Trie or Set for efficient scanning. For long documents, process in chunks.

general_architecture.rule_loading: Load all JSON rules at startup, compile regex patterns once, store in typed Rule[] arrays.
general_architecture.priority_order: Process rules in order: spelling > grammar > clarity > style. Within each, errors before warnings before suggestions.
general_architecture.deduplication: When multiple rules flag the same text span, keep the highest-severity match only.

codebase_mapping.regex_rules: Use createRegexRule() helper from types.ts for all pattern-based rules.
codebase_mapping.nlp_rules: Use NLPRule interface with compromise doc parameter for POS-dependent rules.
codebase_mapping.custom_check_rules: Use RegexRule with custom check() function for complex logic (article a/an, comma splices, sentence fragments).
codebase_mapping.category_files: Create new files in src/rules/categories/ for each code: sentence-structure.ts, verb-tense.ts, nouns-pronouns.ts, articles-determiners.ts, adjectives-adverbs.ts, prepositions.ts, conjunctions.ts, punctuation.ts, spelling-patterns.ts
